explicit_text: My father, Hideo Inaba, immigrated to the United States from Ibaraki Prefecture near Tokyo when he was about ten years old. He worked hard to get through dental school because he wanted a career easier than the farming life his parents had. He opened his own dental practice in Riverside, California, and was well respected in the community. During World War II, he was arrested by the FBI and sent to internment camps, but before that, he generously volunteered free dental care to the poor through church referrals. After the war, he was fortunate to return to his intact dental office thanks to the kindness of his landlady.

explicit_text: My mother, Chiyo Inaba, was born in Washington state and grew up there as a Nisei, the daughter of Japanese immigrants. Her family were big-time farmers who raised milk cows. She met my father while he was still in dental school, and they married before I was born in 1932. During the war, when my father was taken by the FBI, she managed everything, including canceling his dental appointments and moving our family to Los Angeles to stay together before we were sent to Manzanar. She did her best to explain the difficult situation to us children, even though it was hard to understand.

explicit_text: I was born in 1932, right at the height of the Great Depression, so my early childhood was shaped by those tough economic times. My family worked hard to make ends meet, especially my grandparents who farmed from dawn until after dark. The Depression influenced my father's determination to pursue dental school instead of farming like his parents. It was a challenging era, but it taught us resilience and the importance of diversifying our farming to survive.

explicit_text: My father came from Ibaraki Prefecture, which is just outside of Tokyo in Japan. His family were farmers there before they immigrated to the United States. The farming life was very hard, with my grandparents working from early morning until late at night. This background motivated my father to seek a different path in America by going to dental school. Although he left farming behind, the values of hard work from Ibaraki stayed with him.

Tokyo itself was a reference point for where his family came from, but our life in America was centered mostly in places like Riverside and Los Angeles. The connection to Tokyo was part of our heritage, but our experiences were shaped by life here.

explicit_text: My father's farming business expanded to include raising chickens, and he became a Safeway egg producer. He had a contract with Safeway to supply eggs, which helped stabilize his income and made the family quite successful. This relationship with Safeway was an important part of our family's livelihood before the war interrupted everything. It showed how my father diversified his farming to protect against market risks.

He had a contract with that company to supply eggs, which helped stabilize his income and made the family quite successful. This relationship with the grocery chain was an important part of our family's livelihood before the war interrupted everything. It showed how my father diversified his farming to protect against market risks.

explicit_text: Riverside, California, is where my father's dental practice was located and where our family lived before the war. It was a small Japanese American community, very much a minority in the area. My father was well known there, and my mother’s family also farmed nearby. When the war started, we had to leave Riverside, but thanks to friends, our home and office were looked after while we were gone. After the war, we returned to Riverside and found everything intact.

explicit_text: My mother was born and raised in Washington state, making her a Nisei, or second-generation Japanese American. Her family were successful farmers who raised milk cows, which was a different kind of farming than my father's family did. Washington was where my parents’ stories began before they settled in California. It was part of our family's roots and shaped my mother's upbringing before she met my father.

explicit_text: I was about ten years old when the Pearl Harbor attack happened in December 1941, but I don't really remember that day clearly. What I do recall is the tension that followed and how my father was taken by the FBI shortly after. The attack marked the beginning of a very difficult time for our family and the entire Japanese American community. It changed everything, leading to our forced removal and internment.

explicit_text: The FBI came to my father's dental office one day during the war and arrested him after he finished treating a patient. They fingerprinted and photographed him, then sent him to jail before transferring him to internment camps. This was a frightening and confusing time for our family, as my father was taken away and we didn't see him for years. The FBI's actions were part of the government's crackdown on Japanese Americans after Pearl Harbor.
implicit_text: The federal law enforcement agency came to my father's dental office one day during the war and arrested him after he finished treating a patient. They fingerprinted and photographed him, then sent him to jail before transferring him to internment camps. This was a frightening and confusing time for our family, as my father was taken away and we didn't see him for years. Their actions were part of the government's crackdown on Japanese Americans after the attack on Pearl Harbor.

explicit_text: Our Japanese American community in Riverside was quite small and a minority among other groups. My father supported the community by donating to the Japanese community church and volunteering free dental care for anyone in need, regardless of their background. Despite the challenges during the war, including forced removal and internment, the community tried to stay connected and support each other. After the war, friendships with other minority groups, like Mexican American kids at school, helped us feel some solidarity.

explicit_text: Pasadena was home to the wealthy landlady who owned the building where my father had his dental office. When my father was arrested, she and her chauffeur, who was Japanese, came to Riverside to help my mother cover and protect the office equipment. She told us not to worry about rent while my father was gone, showing great kindness and understanding during a difficult time. Pasadena's connection to our family was through her generosity and support.

explicit_text: When my father was taken away, the landlady from Pasadena went with my mother to J.C. Penney to buy sheets to cover all the dental equipment and furniture in the office. This was part of their effort to protect the office while my father was imprisoned. It was a small but meaningful gesture that helped preserve my father's practice for when he returned after the war. J.C. Penney was just where they got the supplies, but it was part of that story of care and support.

explicit_text: After my father was arrested by the FBI, he was first sent to Lordsburg Internment Camp in New Mexico. This was the initial place where he was held before being transferred to other camps. We were separated from him during this time, which was very hard for our family. Lordsburg was just the beginning of a long and difficult journey for him during the war.

explicit_text: Following his time at Lordsburg, my father was sent to Crystal City Internment Camp in Texas. This was one of the places where he was held during the war while our family was at Manzanar. The camps were part of the government's policy to detain Japanese Americans, and Crystal City was where my father spent part of that time separated from us. It was a difficult chapter in our family's history.

explicit_text: My mother, brothers, and I were sent to Manzanar War Relocation Center during the war after my father was taken away. We traveled there by train from Los Angeles, sitting on old bench seats, and I remember people from small farming towns bringing us water and juice during stops. Manzanar was a harsh place, but we stayed together as a family. It was one of the main camps where Japanese Americans were forcibly relocated during World War II.

explicit_text: Before my father was sent to the internment camps, we got to see him through the fence at Santa Anita Assembly Center, which was one of the first emergency centers set up with fencing around it. This was a brief and emotional visit before he was shipped off to camps like Lordsburg and Crystal City. Santa Anita was a temporary holding place for many Japanese Americans early in the war.

explicit_text: After my father was taken, my mother moved with me and my brothers to Los Angeles to stay at my maternal grandparents' house so we could be together before going to Manzanar. Los Angeles was where we regrouped as a family and prepared for the forced relocation. It was a big city compared to Riverside, and it was important for us to stay united during those uncertain times.
